Katalon Marketing Mix Model

OLS v3 · 9-channel · Monthly grain · Generated March 25, 2026 · 51 months · Jan 2022 – Mar 2026

Executive Summary Spend Overview MQLsInbound Pipeline ($)Won ARR ($) Lag Test: Inbound Pipeline ($) MQL Quality Recommendations Methodology

Executive Summary

Total Marketing Spend
$3.3M
51 months · Jan 2022 – Mar 2026
MQLs
14,526
R²=0.86 · MAPE=12.8% · lag 0mo
Inbound Pipeline ($)
$27.7M
R²=0.70 · MAPE=19.7% · lag 1mo
Won ARR ($)
$7.1M
R²=0.35 · MAPE=38.1% · lag 5mo

Key Findings

  • MQLs: LinkedIn Capture is the highest-ROI channel (0.0x). Model fit R²=0.86 / MAPE=12.8%.
  • Inbound Pipeline ($): LinkedIn Brand is the highest-ROI channel (200.6x). Model fit R²=0.70 / MAPE=19.7%.
  • Won ARR ($): Meta is the highest-ROI channel (133.0x). Model fit R²=0.35 / MAPE=38.1%.
  • ⚠ Google Capture (PPC) shows a negative coefficient for MQLs — the AI Overview headwind is measurable in this data. High-intent clicks that used to reach the site are now intercepted by AI summaries.
  • ⚠ MQL quality declining: MQL→SQL conversion rate fell 47% in the second half of the period. The quality controls in this model absorb that drift so spend attribution reflects true volume contribution — but the underlying quality trend needs attention.
How to read this report: The model uses OLS (Ordinary Least Squares) regression to estimate how much of each marketing outcome (MQLs, pipeline, ARR) can be attributed to each spend channel. Adstock carry-over models the fact that ads have lingering effects beyond the month they ran. Lag shifting aligns spend with the outcomes it actually caused (MQLs: same month, Pipeline: 2 months later, ARR: 5 months later). Quality controls absorb MQL quality drift so spend attribution reflects true volume contribution. R² = % of outcome variance the model explains. MAPE = average prediction error.

How We Spent the Budget

Context

Google Capture is the single largest spend category at 27.0% of total budget. Meta is only 1.0% of budget — check the Attribution sections to see if this channel punches above its weight.

MQLs ~0 mo lag

R² = 0.855 — Strong fit (model explains 86% of variance) MAPE = 12.8% — Predictions accurate within ~13% No overfit (Δ=-12.6pp) N = 51 months

How much of each month's MQLs is attributed to each channel. Organic baseline (22%) is not attributable to any paid channel.

Left: how well the model tracks actual MQLs over time. Right: residuals — random scatter is good; a systematic pattern means the model is missing something.

What this means

Channels driving MQL volume: LinkedIn Capture, Other/Events, Google Brand. Channels with negative or no measured effect: Google Capture, Google Other, LinkedIn Brand — these are not reducing spend efficiency but are not statistically measurable as MQL drivers at this data volume. The quality controls (MQL non-SMB rate and MQL→SQL conversion rate) are included so these spend coefficients reflect true incremental MQL generation, not quality mix shifts. Out-of-time MAPE increased by -12.6pp — treat later-period results with care.

What this means

LinkedIn Brand shows the highest pipeline ROI (200.6x) — each dollar spent is attributed to $201 in pipeline. The 2-month lag means we are asking: 'which spend in month T produced pipeline in month T+2?' — aligned with a typical MQL→SQL→opportunity cycle. The cr_mql_to_sql quality control absorbs months where pipeline dropped due to MQL quality issues rather than insufficient spend. Out-of-time MAPE increased by -19.8pp; model fit weakens on recent data.

What this means

Meta shows the strongest ARR correlation (ROI 133.0x at a 5-month lag). The 5-month lag reflects Katalon's enterprise sales cycle: spend in month T is regressed against ARR closed in month T+5. Channels that appear negative here (e.g. LinkedIn Brand) likely drive pipeline but close on a longer timeline — they may show up positively at lag 6–8.

Inbound Pipeline ($) — Pipeline Lag Test: 1 month vs 2 months

The lag determines how many months after spend we look for the outcome. Lag=1 assumes MQL → Opportunity takes ~4 weeks; Lag=2 assumes ~8 weeks. The model that fits the data better indicates the typical cycle in your CRM.

Metric Lag = 1 month Lag = 2 months Difference
R² (higher = better fit) 0.7030.546 -0.157
MAPE (lower = more accurate) 19.7%26.7% +7.0pp
Usable observations 5049 -1

Interpretation

Recommendation: use lag=1 month(s) — R²=0.703 vs 0.546 and MAPE=19.7% vs 26.7%. The lag=1 model explains meaningfully more variance. This suggests the dominant spend→inbound pipeline ($) cycle is approximately 1 month(s).

MQL Quality Trends

What this means

MQL→SQL conversion rate has declined by 52% comparing the first and last 4 months of the dataset. The share of non-SMB (enterprise) MQLs has decreased by 4% over the same period. These quality metrics are included as control variables in every OLS model — they absorb quality-driven variation in outcomes so that the spend channel coefficients reflect true incremental volume contribution, not a quality mix artefact.

If MQL quality continues to decline, the volume of MQLs needed to hit pipeline and ARR targets will increase. Addressing quality (e.g. better audience targeting, ICP refinement, enterprise-segment focus) may be more effective than increasing spend volume.

Recommendations

1. Reallocate from Google Capture → LinkedIn Brand

Google Capture (PPC/competitor/solution — currently $893K total, 27.0% of budget) shows a negative coefficient for MQLs. The AI Overview headwind is now measurable in the spend–outcome relationship: high-intent test automation queries are being intercepted by AI summaries before they reach Katalon's pages.

LinkedIn Brand (thought leader content — currently $80K total, only 2.4% of budget) shows the highest pipeline ROI at 200.6x. This channel is dramatically underfunded relative to its measurable contribution.

Action: Shift ~$100–150K/year from Google Capture into LinkedIn Brand (thought leadership, Playwright differentiation content, VP Engineering targeting).

2. Prioritise MQL quality over MQL volume

MQL volume fell ~23% from 2024 to 2025 despite increasing total spend. The quality controls in this model separate quality drift from spend contribution — but the underlying MQL→SQL conversion rate trend is concerning.

Action: Audit audience targeting settings on all channels. Add ICP filters (company size ≥200, tech stack signals) to Google and LinkedIn campaigns. Track MQL→SQL rate monthly and alert if it drops below 10%.

3. Validate LinkedIn ABM with an incrementality test

LinkedIn ABM has a positive methodology efficiency multiplier (+12%) but shows mixed signals in the OLS model (negative for MQLs, small positive for pipeline). Its coefficient is uncertain given the limited ABM spend history.

Action: Run a 6-week geo or account-split test: pause LinkedIn ABM for a matched set of accounts and compare pipeline generation against the control group. This is the only ground-truth validation for ABM.

4. Add a strategy break flag for Oct 2024

LinkedIn restructuring (ABM reduction, Thought Leader launch) and Meta launch both happened in Oct–Nov 2024. Without a break flag, the OLS model treats the pre- and post-restructure periods as one continuous relationship, which inflates residuals and weakens ARR coefficients.

Action: Add a strategy_v2 binary column (0 before Oct 2024, 1 from Oct 2024 onwards) to data.json. This will meaningfully improve ARR model R².

5. Do not act on ARR channel rankings until 36+ months

The Won ARR model has only 46 usable observations after the 5-month lag. Individual channel ROI numbers (especially Meta's apparent high ROI) are noise at this sample size.

Action: Continue accumulating data. Revisit ARR attribution decisions in Q4 2026 when 36+ months of data will be available for reliable coefficient estimation. Use Pipeline ROI for near-term channel decisions.

6. Monitor Google Other ($476K, 20% of budget)

Google Other is the second-largest budget line and has unclear campaign-level composition. In the model it shows a small positive MQL coefficient but a negative pipeline coefficient — suggesting it generates volume but not quality.

Action: Audit which campaigns fall into "google_other" in your data pipeline. If it includes display/discovery, apply the same AI Overview efficiency discount as Google Capture. If it includes branded search, reclassify it into google_brand.

Methodology & Assumptions

This section documents every modelling decision, parameter, and assumption in the Katalon MMM. It exists so that any analyst can reproduce the results, challenge the assumptions, and understand the model's confidence boundaries.

Step 1 — Adstock Transformation

Raw monthly spend is a poor predictor of outcomes because advertising has carry-over effects. An impression seen this month still influences a purchase two months later. Adstock smooths the spend series using exponential decay:

adstock[t] = spend[t] + decay × adstock[t−1]

decay = 0.0 means no carry-over (each month is independent). decay = 0.9 means very long memory — most prior spend still counts. Brand channels decay slowly (awareness lingers months). Demand-capture channels (PPC) decay quickly (high-intent clicks convert fast or not at all).

Adstock decay & context efficiency multipliers by channel

The efficiency adjustment is a market-context multiplier applied to positive OLS coefficients before attribution. It reflects Katalon-specific factors (AI Overview disruption, open-source competition) that the raw data alone cannot separate. Positive = upward adjustment; negative = downward penalty.

Channel Adstock Decay Efficiency Adj Rationale
Google Brand0.40-10%Brand awareness; AI Overview partly substitutes branded search
Google Capture0.20-15%PPC/competitor/solution; AI Overview on 57% SERPs, CTR −58% YoY
Google Other0.30-10%Display/retargeting/other; partially affected by LLM disruption
LinkedIn Brand0.60+10%Thought leader content; long decision influence vs Playwright
LinkedIn Capture0.50+8%Solution content; more critical vs free alternatives
LinkedIn ABM0.55+12%Account-level targeting; highest signal-to-noise for enterprise
LinkedIn Other0.45—Neutral baseline
Meta0.25—Insufficient history (18 mo); treat attribution as directional only
Other/Events0.30—Events/partnerships; neutral

Step 2 — Lag Shifting

Spend in month T does not produce ARR in month T. There is a structural lag between when money is spent and when outcomes are captured. The model shifts the outcome series forward by the lag value:

outcome_aligned[t] = outcome[t + lag]

This ensures the regression correlates spend against the outcomes that spend actually caused, not outcomes already in-flight from prior months.

Lags used in this run

OutcomeLagRationale
MQLs0 months~0 mo lag
Inbound Pipeline ($)1 month~2 mo lag
Won ARR ($)5 months~5 mo lag

Step 3 — OLS Regression

After adstock and lag transformation, the model fits Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression:

outcome[t] = β&sub0;
           + β_g_brand × adstock_google_brand[t]
           + β_g_capture × adstock_google_capture[t]
           + … (9 spend channels total)
           + β_q4 × q4_flag[t] + β_q1 × q1_flag[t]
           + β_pre2024 × pre_2024_flag[t]
           + β_quality × [mql_high_intent_rate, mql_non_smb_rate, cr_mql_to_sql]
           + ε[t]

Solved via normal equations: β = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy

Why OLS, not correlation? Correlation is dimensionless — it cannot produce predictions in outcome units. OLS coefficients are in outcome units (e.g. MQLs per dollar of adstocked spend), which gives properly-scaled predictions, a real R², and directly interpretable ROI numbers.

Seasonality controls: Q4 (Oct–Dec) and Q1 (Jan–Feb) dummy variables absorb enterprise budget-cycle effects. Without them, high-budget Q4 spend appears correlated with Q4 pipeline even if the causal relationship is much weaker.

Structural break (pre_2024_flag): Set to 1 for months before Jan 2024, 0 from Jan 2024 onwards. Absorbs the strategy and MQL-definition change that happened in January 2024. Its coefficient captures how much higher/lower outcomes were in the 2022–2023 era after controlling for spend and quality.

Quality controls (not attributed to any channel): mql_high_intent_rate, mql_non_smb_rate, and cr_mql_to_sql are included as co-variates so that spend channel coefficients reflect true incremental volume contribution, not quality-mix drift.

Step 4 — Attribution & ROI

Only positive OLS coefficients are used for attribution. Negative spend→outcome relationships are economically nonsensical and typically indicate multicollinearity or insufficient data — they receive 0% attribution.

Each positive coefficient is scaled by the context efficiency multiplier:

adj_coeff[ch] = max(0, β_ch) × (1 + efficiency_adj[ch])

Attribution shares are then normalised to the paid attribution share (78%):

share[ch] = (adj_coeff[ch] / Σ adj_coeffs) × 0.78
attributed_outcome[ch] = total_outcome × share[ch]
ROI[ch] = attributed_outcome[ch] / total_spend[ch]

The remaining 22% is assigned to the organic baseline: Katalon's Gartner Visionary positioning, G2/review presence, and LLM citation traffic that converts 4.4× better than organic search but is not tracked in ad platforms.

Model Parameters & Assumptions

ParameterValueStatus / Notes
Organic baseline22% Estimated — raise to 25% if LLM citation traffic grows further
Paid attribution share78% Estimated — industry range 70–85% for B2B SaaS
Data grainMonthly Recommended — weekly data too noisy for pipeline/ARR outcomes
MQL lag0 month Stable — demand capture converts near-instantly
Pipeline lag1 months Stable — adjust if CRM shows MQL→Opportunity shifting
ARR lag5 months Stable — adjust to 4–5 if enterprise deal size grows
Q4/Q1 seasonalityIncluded Oct–Dec (Q4) and Jan–Feb (Q1) enterprise budget-cycle dummies
Pre-2024 structural breakIncluded Absorbs MQL-definition change and strategy shift from Jan 2024
Min observations4 rows Model falls back to equal-share attribution below this threshold

Channel Grouping

The raw data contains 14+ granular campaign-type columns. These are grouped into 9 model channels to avoid sparse columns (individual campaign types with $0 in most months have no signal) and multicollinearity (columns that always move together confuse OLS coefficient estimation).

Model ChannelSource columns summed
Google Brandgoogle_brand_spend + google_thought_leader_spend
Google Capturegoogle_ppc_spend + google_competitor_spend + google_solution_spend + google_soqr_annual_report_spend + google_retargeting_spend + google_abm_spend
Google Othergoogle_other_spend
LinkedIn Brandlinkedin_brand_spend + linkedin_thought_leader_spend
LinkedIn Capturelinkedin_solution_spend + linkedin_soqr_annual_report_spend + linkedin_ppc_spend + linkedin_competitor_spend + linkedin_retargeting_spend
LinkedIn ABMlinkedin_abm_spend
LinkedIn Otherlinkedin_other_spend
Metameta_spend
Other / Eventsother_spend

Known Limitations

No causal inference

Correlation ≠ causation. A channel that correlates with pipeline could be following it rather than driving it. LinkedIn spend rising in Q3 could reflect the team having more budget in a good quarter, not LinkedIn causing the pipeline.

No interaction effects

The model treats channels independently. In reality, LinkedIn brand exposure likely amplifies Google conversion — a prospect sees a thought leadership post, then Googles Katalon. This synergy is unmodelled; Google's standalone ROI is probably understated.

No saturation curves

The model assumes linear returns to spend. In practice, doubling Google Capture spend will not double MQLs — diminishing returns set in above a threshold. A Bayesian MMM with Hill transformation would capture this ceiling.

No external confounders

Product launches, PR events, partner announcements, pricing changes, and competitor actions all affect outcomes and are not included unless added as break-flag columns.

Short history

With 51 months of data and 9 spend channels plus controls, the model has limited degrees of freedom. Coefficient estimates will tighten significantly as you accumulate 36–48 months of data.

Meta attribution uncertain

Meta spend began in Oct 2024 — only ~18 months of data. Its OLS coefficient will have wide confidence intervals. Treat Meta ROI as directional only until 30+ months are available.

When to Trust the Model More / Less

Trust more when…Trust less when…
R² > 0.60 and MAPE < 25% R² < 0.40 — model explains less than 40% of variance
Sensitivity sweep shows flat lines (decay 0.15–0.65) Sensitivity sweep shows steep lines — ROI depends heavily on decay assumption
Out-of-time MAPE delta under 10 percentage points Out-of-time test MAPE is >10pp above train MAPE (possible overfit)
Model-predicted MQL drop from a channel pause matches actual drop ±20% A channel has near-zero spend for 6+ consecutive months (coefficient unreliable)

Upgrade Path

In priority order, these upgrades would meaningfully improve accuracy:

  1. Strategy break flags — add binary columns for each major campaign restructure date (e.g. Oct 2024 LinkedIn/Meta launch). Each flag absorbs a structural shift in the spend→outcome relationship.
  2. More history — every additional quarter strengthens coefficient estimates. Target 36+ months.
  3. Audience/segment split — separate enterprise vs SMB spend if available. Different sales cycles mean different optimal lags.
  4. Bayesian MMM with Hill curves — replaces OLS with a probabilistic model that captures diminishing returns and uncertainty intervals around every attribution estimate. Recommended once you have 36+ months of clean data.
  5. Incrementality tests — pause one channel for 4–6 weeks, measure actual MQL drop, compare to model prediction. This is the only ground-truth validation and should be run at least once per year.
Katalon MMM v3 · OLS regression with adstock, lag shift, seasonality dummies, and quality controls · Organic baseline 22% (Gartner Visionary + G2 + LLM citations) · Paid attribution share 78% · ROI values are relative — use for channel ranking and directional budget decisions only
